questions.question: Explain the concept of Type Wrappers in Java. Why are they needed when primitive data types already exist?
keyPoints: Type Wrappers are classes that encapsulate primitive types as objects
Needed because primitives cannot be used in object-only contexts like Collections, generics, and synchronized blocks
Enable primitives to participate in OOP features like polymorphism
All wrapper classes are in java.lang package and are automatically imported

questions.question: Differentiate between Autoboxing and Unboxing with code examples.
keyPoints: Autoboxing: automatic conversion of primitive to wrapper object (e.g., Integer i = 10;)
Unboxing: automatic conversion of wrapper object to primitive (e.g., int x = new Integer(10);)
Both are performed by Java compiler at compile-time
Helps write cleaner code without explicit casting

questions.question: What is Integer caching in Java? Explain with an example showing the importance of == vs equals() when comparing wrapper objects.
keyPoints: Java caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127
valueOf() and autoboxing return cached objects within range
== compares references, equals() compares values
Using == for object comparison can lead to unexpected results outside cache range

questions.question: Write a Java program to demonstrate the difference between parseInt() and valueOf() methods of Integer class.
keyPoints: parseInt() returns primitive int
valueOf() returns Integer object
Both parse String but return different types
Demonstrate with code showing return types and caching behavior

questions.question: Explain why wrapper classes are immutable. What are the advantages of immutability in wrapper classes?
keyPoints: Once created, wrapper objects cannot be modified
Any operation returns a new wrapper object
Enables thread safety without synchronization
Allows safe use in HashMap/HashSet collections
Facilitates caching of frequently used values

questions.question: What are the common methods available in all wrapper classes? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: xxxValue() - converts to primitive types
toString() - returns String representation
equals() - compares object values
valueOf() - creates wrapper from String or primitive
parseXxx() - parses String to primitive

questions.question:
Create a comprehensive Java program that demonstrates:
1. Autoboxing and Unboxing
2. Integer caching behavior with == and equals()
3. Converting between different numeric types using xxxValue() methods
4. Handling NullPointerException when unboxing null wrapper objects
keyPoints: Show autoboxing: Integer i = 100;
Show unboxing: int x = i;
Compare cached vs non-cached Integer with == and equals()
Use intValue(), doubleValue() for type conversion
Use try-catch for NPE during null unboxing

questions.question: Explain the Number class hierarchy in Java. How do the methods defined in Number class facilitate polymorphism and generic programming? Give suitable examples.
keyPoints: Number is abstract parent class for Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double
Defines methods: byteValue(), shortValue(), intValue(), longValue(), floatValue(), doubleValue()
Enables writing generic code that works with any numeric type
Demonstrate with a method that accepts Number and extracts values

questions.question: Write a Java program that uses a generic method to perform arithmetic operations (add, multiply) on any numeric wrapper type using the Number class and its methods. Explain how polymorphism enables this.
keyPoints: Create generic method accepting Number type
Use doubleValue() or other methods for uniform processing
Demonstrate with Integer, Double, Float arguments
Explain compile-time type safety and runtime polymorphism benefits
